title: Swiss roll
subtitle: A classic rolled sponge with jam and cream
excerpt: Swiss roll is a thin fatless sponge baked quickly in a rectangular tin, then rolled while still warm around a filling of jam and whipped cream. The technique depends on speed: the sponge must be rolled before it cools or it will crack. Once mastered, the method works for any rolled sponge variation.
sourceNotes: The rolled sponge filled with jam appears in British baking manuals from the mid-nineteenth century. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, includes a recipe for Swiss roll using a fatless sponge. The name has no clear Swiss origin and may be a Victorian marketing term. The technique of rolling a hot sponge to set the shape before unrolling and filling is consistent across historical and modern recipes.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The unfilled sponge can be baked a day ahead and stored rolled in its sugar-dusted paper. Fill on the day of serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 10 to 12 minutes only; overbaking makes the sponge crack when rolled

content.text: Speed matters at two points in this recipe: when folding the flour so you do not knock out the air, and when rolling the sponge immediately as it comes from the oven. Have a sheet of baking paper dusted with caster sugar ready on the worktop before you even put the tin in the oven. The sponge starts to cool and stiffen within two or three minutes of leaving the heat, at which point it becomes brittle.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Grease a 33 x 23 cm swiss roll tin and line with baking paper. In a large bowl, beat 3 large eggs, 75 g caster sugar, and 1 tsp vanilla extract with a hand mixer on high speed for 4 to 5 minutes until very thick and pale: when you lift the beaters, the mixture should fall in a ribbon that holds its shape for several seconds. Sift in 75 g plain flour and fold with a large metal spoon using wide, gentle strokes until just combined. Pour into the prepared tin and tilt to level. Bake for 10 to 12 minutes until the sponge springs back when pressed lightly.

Lay a sheet of baking paper on the work surface and dust generously with caster sugar. Immediately turn the sponge out onto it. Peel off the lining paper. Using the paper underneath as a guide, roll the sponge from one short end, keeping the paper inside. Leave to cool completely, rolled, for at least 20 minutes. Unroll carefully. Spread with 4 tablespoons of jam, then add whipped cream if using. Re-roll, this time without the paper. Chill for 30 minutes before slicing.

items.solution: Either the sponge was rolled after it had cooled, or it was overbaked. Roll while still hot from the oven. If it was in the oven for more than 12 minutes it will be drier and less pliable.

items.problem: The sponge is flat and dense
items.solution: The eggs were not whisked enough, or the flour was folded in too vigorously. The whisked mixture should be pale, very thick, and hold a ribbon for at least 5 seconds. Sift the flour twice and fold with a light hand.
